---
title: "Draft: placement of `vis_lm_assumptions()` context in the vignette"
output: html_document
---

## Factual mechanism checked

- `vis_lm_assumptions()` fits the corresponding linear model and computes
  internally studentised residuals with `rstandard()`.
- The diagnostic graphic has four panels: histogram with normal density,
  standardised residuals vs. fitted values, normal Q--Q plot, and either
  scale-location or standardised residuals vs. leverage.
- The outer title has two rows: normality checks in row 1; variance checks
  in row 2.

## Proposed structure

The `vis_lm_assumptions()` context belongs in the existing section

```md
# The general linear model assumption diagnostics `vis_lm_assumptions()` {#assumptions}
```

not in the later decision-tree subsection. This section should explain what
the first diagnostic panel is and what is displayed in its title. The following
decision-logic section should then explain which of those displayed checks
actually route the test selection.

Proposed local structure:

```md
# Decision logic

[overview figure and high-level routing by input class]

# The general linear model assumption diagnostics `vis_lm_assumptions()` {#assumptions}

[GLM assumptions shared by Student's t-test, Fisher's ANOVA, simple regression]

[vis_lm_assumptions(): fitted model, residuals, four panels, outer title]

[one bridge sentence: not all displayed checks enter routing]

# Decision logic in the linear model family {#routing}

[short transition to the following subsections]

## Numeric response and categorical predictor: comparing central tendencies {#numeric-categoric}

[decision tree and actual routing: group-size gate, Shapiro--Wilk, Levene]

## Both variables numeric: regression or Spearman rank correlation

[regression branch and explicit Spearman alternative]
```

## Exact insertion point

Replace the current incomplete `vis_lm_assumptions()` explanation in
`vignettes/visStatistics.Rmd`, lines 149--163, with the block below.

Then remove the current `### Graphical output` block at lines 169--173,
because the same information is then already handled in the
`vis_lm_assumptions()` section.

Also remove the repeated central-tendency GLM sentence at lines 179--180:

```md
Student's t-test, Fisher's ANOVA all belong to the general linear model framework (#glm)) and thus share a common set of assumptions.
Therefore the test selection for [tests of central tendencies](#numeric-categoric) is steered by checking the fitted model residuals in regard to these assumptions.
```

## Draft replacement block

```md
The function `vis_lm_assumptions()` provides the diagnostic panel used
for the central-tendency branch with numeric responses and for the
simple-regression branch. It fits the corresponding linear model and computes
internally studentised residuals with `rstandard()`.

The diagnostic graphic is displayed before the selected test or
regression result. It contains four panels: a histogram with normal
density overlay, standardised residuals versus fitted values, a normal
Q--Q plot, and a fourth plot depending on the branch. For grouped
central-tendency analyses, the fourth plot is a scale-location plot; for
simple regression, it is a standardised residuals-versus-leverage plot.

The outer title reports the displayed assumption checks and their
$p$-values in two rows. The first row reports residual-normality checks:
Shapiro--Wilk and Anderson--Darling. The second row reports variance
checks: Brown--Forsythe Levene-type and Bartlett for grouped
central-tendency analyses, or Breusch--Pagan for simple regression.

Not all displayed checks enter automated routing. The following section
describes which checks are used for test selection.
```

## Resulting flow around the insertion

```md
# The general linear model assumption diagnostics `vis_lm_assumptions()` {#assumptions}

Student's t-test, Fisher's ANOVA, and simple linear regression all belong
to the general linear model framework [@Thompson:2015] and thus share a
common set of assumptions: the expected value of the response is a
linear function of the predictors, the error terms are independent and
normally distributed with expectation 0, and the error variance is
constant at $\sigma^2$ (see [Appendix A](#glm)).

The function `vis_lm_assumptions()` provides the diagnostic panel used
for the central-tendency branch with numeric responses and for the
simple-regression branch. It fits the corresponding linear model and computes
internally studentised residuals with `rstandard()`.

The diagnostic graphic is displayed before the selected test or
regression result. It contains four panels: a histogram with normal
density overlay, standardised residuals versus fitted values, a normal
Q--Q plot, and a fourth plot depending on the branch. For grouped
central-tendency analyses, the fourth plot is a scale-location plot; for
simple regression, it is a standardised residuals-versus-leverage plot.

The outer title reports the displayed assumption checks and their
$p$-values in two rows. The first row reports residual-normality checks:
Shapiro--Wilk and Anderson--Darling. The second row reports variance
checks: Brown--Forsythe Levene-type and Bartlett for grouped
central-tendency analyses, or Breusch--Pagan for simple regression.

Not all displayed checks enter automated routing. The following section
describes which checks are used for test selection.

# Decision logic in the linear model family {#routing}

In the following subsections, we detail the decision logic following the
structure of the overview figure.

## Numeric response and categorical predictor: comparing central tendencies {#numeric-categoric}

The following figure expands the central-tendency branch.
```

## Repetition avoided

- The `vis_lm_assumptions()` section explains displayed diagnostics.
- The `Numeric response and categorical predictor` section explains routing.
- The GLM assumption sentence appears once, before `vis_lm_assumptions()`.
- The post-hoc paragraph stays in the central-tendency section because it
  belongs to the selected-result plot, not the assumption-diagnostic panel.
